Where To Stay In Tokyo 2026
Where should you stay in Tokyo? In this section, I’ll give you my picks for the best, most convenient and most interesting neighborhoods (and hotels) in Tokyo as of January 2026.
![The exterior of the Palace Hotel Tokyo with elements of the curved metal and glass frontage reflected in the moat of the neighboring Imperial Palace.](https://photos.smugmug.com/Tokyo/n-S6Q5Pp/Where-To-Stay-In-Tokyo/i-pP78qfC/0/LKj4TsCKtH6Jd6Z8ShTG3jQfB3TsTdLtThqFGgxhx/L/Palace%20Hotel%20Tokyo%201-L.png "Palace Hotel Tokyo exterior")
Palace Hotel Tokyo © Chris Rowthorn
## The Takeaway
As of January 2026, the following are the best, most convenient areas to stay in Tokyo, listed in order of preference. This is based on living in Tokyo for several months, writing several guidebooks about the city for Lonely Planet, and staying in hotels all over the city (as well as getting feedback from my consulting and tour clients):
1. Tokyo Station and Marunouchi Area
2. Shinjuku
3. Shibuya
4. Roppongi
5. Ginza and Hibiya
6. Shimbashi, Shiodome and Hamamatsucho
7. Ebisu, Meguro and Daikanyama
8. Shinagawa
At the end of this page, you’ll find a map that shows exactly where all these places are located.

## The Details
Tokyo is a huge city. In fact, it’s better to think of Tokyo as a group of cities connected by a great transport system. Thus, it’s really important to choose a good area in which to stay.
There are three criteria I look at when ranking the best places to stay in Tokyo:
1. It must be near the Yamanote Line, which is the most important form of transport in the city,
2. It must have lots of good restaurants and shops within easy walking distance,
3. The area must be attractive.
I’ll go through the best places to stay one-by-one below.
Recommended Mid Range Tokyo Hotels

[![Marunouchi Hotel](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E)](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/marunouchi-hotel.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTMidRangeTokyoHotelGrid-07)[Marunouchi Hotel](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/marunouchi-hotel.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTMidRangeTokyoHotelGrid-08)Tokyo Station[More Info](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/marunouchi-hotel.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTMidRangeTokyoHotelGrid-09)

## The Best Places To Stay In Tokyo
Here is my list of the best places to stay in Tokyo, listed in order of preference, along with my favorite hotels in each district.
![View of the skyscrapers surrounding Marunouchi Square, as two people sit and relax, with the distinctive red-brick Tokyo Station building to the left.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Tokyo Station and Marunouchi area")
Tokyo Station and Marunouchi area © Chris Rowthorn
### [Tokyo Station/Marunouchi Area](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyo-station-and-marunouchi/)
The Tokyo Station/Marunouchi area, the city’s main transport hub, is the best place to stay in Tokyo. This is usually where I stay when I’m in Tokyo. You’ve got the most important train station, including the terminal station of the Tokaido shinkansen line (for easy access to Kyoto etc). And, within easy walking distance you’ve got all the city’s main department stores in Ginza, Nihombashi etc. And, the area is literally awash with restaurants. Nearby, you’ve got the Imperial Palace and adjoining parks. And, best of all, the streets around here (especially in Marunouchi) are broad and pleasant.
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** transport hub of Tokyo, great shops, restaurants and hotels
* **Negatives:** can feel a little dead in the evening
* **Best for:** almost anyone, especially those looking to do a lot of exploring
Here are a couple of pics to give you a feeling for the Tokyo Station and Marunouchi area that I took in February of 2025.
![Daytime view of the Tokyo Station Yaesu Side, Chiyoda-Ku, Tokyo, Japan, with the station Granroof, designed by Helmut Jahn, visible to the right, and some parked vehichles in the foreground.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Tokyo Station Yaesu Side")
Tokyo Station Yaesu Side in early spring 2025 © Chris Rowthorn
![An evening view of the historic red-brick Tokyo Station illuminated with warm lights, standing in contrast to the towering modern glass skyscrapers behind it. In the foreground, a spacious paved plaza is filled with pedestrians, bordered by a patch of green lawn on the left under a twilight sky.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Tokyo Station Marunouchi side in the evening")
Tokyo Station Marunouchi side in the evening © Chris Rowthorn
#### Best Hotel Picks For The Tokyo Station/Marunouchi Area
Our top hotel picks in the Tokyo Station/Marunouchi area are:
![A spacious luxury hotel room with high ceilings and classic European decor. Two twin beds with tall, tufted grey headboards take up the right side, dressed in white linens with deep purple runners and patterned bolster pillows. A gold chandelier with multiple lampshades hangs centrally. On the left, a dark wood dresser holds a flat-screen TV. A lime-green armchair sits near a tall window draped with sheer and heavy curtains at the far end of the room, standing on a carpet with a geometric maroon and beige pattern.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "The Tokyo Station Hotel guest room")
The Tokyo Station Hotel guest room © Chris Rowthorn
* **Local Luxury:** [Tokyo Station Hotel](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyo-station-hotel-tokyo/) - Located directly above Tokyo Station (in the same building), this hotel is superb. There’s no hint of train noise and the rooms are spacious. Despite the huge rooms, impeccable service and great concierges, the price is extremely reasonable. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyo-station-hotel-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/tokyo-station.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=305312&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
![Luxurious suite room in the Shangri-La Hotel with large corner windows, sofa and armchair, subtle lighting, and views of the city.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Shangri-La Tokyo corner suite")
Shangri-La Tokyo corner suite © Chris Rowthorn
* **International Luxury:** [Shangri-La Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/shangri-la-hotel-tokyo/) - I love the Shangri-La Tokyo. It’s in a tower right above Tokyo Station with eye-popping views in every direction. The hotel has the feeling of a secret sanctuary in the sky and the rooms, restaurants and spa are sublime. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/shangri-la-hotel-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/shangri-la-tokyo.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=148855&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
![Luxurious guest room in the Mandarin Oriental hotel in Tokyo, with two large beds, wall-length wooden desk, and incredible views of the city skyline.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Mandarin Oriental Tokyo guest room")
Mandarin Oriental Tokyo guest room © Chris Rowthorn
* **International Luxury:** [Mandarin Oriental, Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/mandarin-oriental-tokyo-tokyo-station-marunouchi-district-tokyo/) - Located high atop a tower in Nihombashi, within walking distance of Tokyo Station, the Mandarin Oriental Tokyo is arguably the best hotel in Tokyo. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/mandarin-oriental-tokyo-tokyo-station-marunouchi-district-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/mandarin-oriental-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=3849629&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
![Double corner guest room in the Hotel Ryumeikan Tokyo, Japan, with large double bed facing a freestanding TV and wall-length corner windows overlooking illuminated night view of the city.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='534'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20534'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Hotel Ryumeikan Tokyo double corner guest room")
Hotel Ryumeikan Tokyo
* **Mid-Range:** [Hotel Ryumeikan Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/hotel-ryumeikan-tokyo/) - The Hotel Ryumeikan Tokyo is the best midrange hotel in Tokyo and I usually stay here when in the city. Located directly across the street from Tokyo Station, this hotel is a great value. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/hotel-ryumeikan-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/ryumeikan-tokyo.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=164430&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
If you’re a traveler who wants the conveniences of home, check out the excellent [Ascott Marunouchi Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/ascott-marunouchi-hotel/), which offers great serviced apartments. And if you’re on a budget or traveling with the family, check out the [karaska hotel colors Tokyo Yaesu](https://trulytokyo.com/karaksa-hotel-colors-tokyo-yaesu/), which offers connecting rooms and reasonable rates just steps from Tokyo Station.
For a list of my favorite hotels in this area, see my [Tokyo Station/Marunouchi Places To Stay section](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyo-station-and-marunouchi/).
![View of tall contemporary buildings in West Shinjuku, Tokyo, from an elevated walkway, on a bright, sunny days, including the distintive Mode Gakuen Cocoon Tower.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Towers of West Shinjuku")
Towers of West Shinjuku © Chris Rowthorn
### [Shinjuku](https://trulytokyo.com/shinjuku/)
Shinjuku is a close second as the best place to stay in Tokyo (and it may rank first if you love nightlife and don’t mind crowds). This is one of the city’s biggest transport hubs and it’s on the all-important Yamanote Line. There are several huge department stores, huge electronics shops, literally thousands of restaurants, and Tokyo’s best bookshop: Kinokuniya. Note that the east side of the station is chaotic and crowded (but fun) and the west side is quieter and calmer. But if you really don’t like crowds, consider somewhere quieter like the [Tokyo Station/Marunouchi area](https://trulytokyo.com/Tokyo-station-and-marunouchi/) or [Ebisu, Meguro or Daikanyama](https://trulytokyo.com/ebisu-daikanyama-and-meguro/).
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** great transport connections, amazing shopping and dining
* **Negatives:** east side can be a bit overwhelming for kids
* **Best for:** young people, foodies, shoppers and bar-hoppers
Here are a couple of pics to give an idea of the street level vibe of Shinjuku. First, here’s West Shinjuku, which is the calmer side:
![View from Shinjuku Station in Tokyo, Japan, of one of West Shinjuku’s main streets with traffic and lined with large buildings, looking towards the Park Hyatt tower.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "West Shinuku, looking toward the Park Hyatt tower")
West Shinuku, looking toward the Park Hyatt tower © Chris Rowthorn
![View of the West Shinjuku train tracks towards the NTT Docomo Yoyogi Building on the left and the Hotel Century Southern Tower on the right.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Train tracks of Shinjuku Station (West Shinjuku)")
Train tracks of Shinjuku Station (West Shinjuku) with Hotel Century Southern Tower visible on right © Chris Rowthorn
Here’s East Shinjuku, which is the busier nightlife side:
![Daytime image of Godzilla Road with Godzilla himself peeping up over the side of the Hotel Gracery in East Shinjuku, Tokyo.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "East Shinjuku with Gracery Hotel")
East Shinjuku with Gracery Hotel in middle © Chris Rowthorn
![Daytime Yasukuni Street scene in the Kabukicho area of East Shinjuku, Tokyo, with many shops and restaurants.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "East Shinuku street scene")
East Shinuku street scene © Chris Rowthorn
#### Best Hotel Picks For Shinjuku
Our top hotel picks in Shinjuku are:
![Corner guest room in the Kimpton Shinjuku hotel, Tokyo, Japan, with contemporary interior design with double bed, L-shaped leather sofa, and corner seating area.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='566'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20566'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Corner guest room in the Kimpton Shinjuku hotel, Tokyo")
Kimpton Shinjuku
* **Luxury:** [Kimpton Shinjuku](https://trulytokyo.com/kimpton-shinjuku/) - The Kimpton is a reasonably priced and very stylish luxury hotel on the west side of Shinjuku, within walking distance of the convenient Shinjuku Station. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/kimpton-shinjuku/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/kimpton-shinjuku-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=13349942&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
![Corner guest room on the upper floors of the Hotel Century Southern Tower, Shinjuku, Tokyo, Japan, with chaise longue and armchair, looking out on the city skyscrapers and large park area.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='534'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20534'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Corner guest room in the Hotel Century Southern Tower, Shinjuku")
Hotel Century Southern Tower
* **Mid-Range:** [Hotel Century Southern Tower](https://trulytokyo.com/hotel-century-southern-tower-tokyo/) - With one of the best locations in Tokyo, great views, comfortable rooms and reasonable rates, the Hotel Century Southern Tower is one of the best midrange hotels in the city. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/hotel-century-southern-tower-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/century-southern-tower.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=648748&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
![Twin guest room in the Sotetsu Fresa Inn Higashi Shinjuku, Tokyo, Japan, with padded headboards and strip lighting above the pillows, brown carpet with sprial geometric patterns.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='534'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20534'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Twin guest room in the Sotetsu Fresa Inn Higashi Shinjuku")
Sotetsu Fresa Inn Higashi Shinjuku
* **Budget:** [Sotetsu Fresa Inn Higashi Shinjuku](https://trulytokyo.com/hotel-sunroute-higashi-shinjuku-tokyo/) - Located in East Shinjuku, directly next to a subway station, the Sotetsu Fresa Inn Higashi Shinjuku is an excellent value and well-run business hotel. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/hotel-sunroute-higashi-shinjuku-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/sunroute-higashi-shinjuku.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=76398&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
![Family room in the Mimaru Tokyo Shinjuku West hotel, Tokyo, Japan, with two sets of twin beds, long dining table with sofa seating, and contemporary update on traditional minimal Japanese style.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='534'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20534'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Family room in the Mimaru Tokyo Shinjuku West hotel, Tokyo")
Mimaru Tokyo Shinjuku West
* **Family:** [Mimaru Shinjuku West](https://trulytokyo.com/mimaru-tokyo-shinjuku-west/) - The Mimaru Tokyo Shinjuku West is THE BEST FAMILY HOTEL IN TOKYO. It’s got a great location in west Shinjuku, which is our favorite place to stay in the city, and it’s got a variety of large rooms (apartments?) with simple cooking facilities and comfortable beds. And the rates are very reasonable. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/mimaru-tokyo-shinjuku-west/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/mimaru-tokyo-shinjuku-west.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) or [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/en-gb/mimaru-tokyo-shinjuku-west/hotel/tokyo-jp.html?CID=1741664;tag=TT-where-to-stay).
You may also want to check out the [Hundred Stay Tokyo Shinjuku](https://trulytokyo.com/hundred-stay-tokyo-shinjuku/), which offers great family rooms at amazingly reasonable rates.
For my list of Shinjuku’s best hotels in all price ranges, see my [Shinjuku Places To Stay section](https://trulytokyo.com/shinjuku/).
Recommended Luxury Hotels In Tokyo
[![Mandarin Oriental](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E)](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/mandarin-oriental-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-01)[Mandarin Oriental](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/mandarin-oriental-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-02)Tokyo Station[More Info](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/mandarin-oriental-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-03)
[![The Peninsula](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E)](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/the-peninsula-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-04)[The Peninsula](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/the-peninsula-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-05)Tokyo Station[More Info](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/the-peninsula-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-06)
[![Ritz-Carlton](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E)](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/the-ritz-carlton-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-07)[Ritz-Carlton](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/the-ritz-carlton-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-08)Roppongi[More Info](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/the-ritz-carlton-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-09)
[![Conrad Tokyo](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='auto'%20height='auto'%20viewBox='0%200%20auto%20auto'%3E%3C/svg%3E)](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/conrad-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-10)[Conrad Tokyo](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/conrad-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-11)Shiodome[More Info](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/conrad-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TTLuxuryTokyoHotelsGrid-12)
![Aerial view looking down on the famous Shibuya Crossing and illuminated buildings at twilight in Tokyo, Japan.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='534'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20534'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Shibuya in the evening")
Shibuya in the evening © [f11photo / Shutterstock.com](https://www.shutterstock.com/image-photo/shibuya-crossing-top-view-twilight-tokyo-666197236)
### [Shibuya](https://trulytokyo.com/shibuya/)
Shibuya is another huge shopping and transport hub on the west side of the Yamanote Line. It’s pretty similar to Shinjuku and the reasons I rate this highly are the same as for Shinjuku: convenient transport connections (including the Yamanote Line), plenty of shops and restaurants, and lots of attractions. It’s just slightly less busy than Shinjuku and a bit more youth oriented, in case that matters. My kids love this area and I’m more comfortable staying here with them than I would be in Shinjuku.
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** fun, trendy, great shopping and dining
* **Negatives:** station area is almost always crowded
* **Best for:** families, young people, shoppers
Here are some photos I took in the spring of 2025 that give a feeling for the streets of Shibuya:
![The Finger Ave crossroads in a bright early morning view of Shibuya in early spring, as a handful of people pass the Parco and Moncler stores.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20width='800'%20height='600'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20600'%3E%3C/svg%3E "Shibuya street view")

### [Roppongi](https://trulytokyo.com/roppongi-and-akasaka/)
Roppongi is another great place to stay. In fact, I am sure there are plenty of Tokyoites and regular visitors who would rank this at #1. The only thing that stops me from doing so is the fact that Roppongi is not on the Yamanote Line. I do a lot of moving around when I’m in Tokyo, so being on the loop line is important. If you don’t plan to do so much bouncing around, then Roppongi might well be your first choice. If you rate great dining, exciting nightlife and plenty of attractions highly, then you should strongly consider staying in Roppongi. This is the sexiest and most cosmopolitan part of the city. It’s where the big money (both Japanese and foreign) eats and drinks.
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** sexy, glamorous, dining and nightlife
* **Negatives:** not on the Yamanote Loop Line
* **Best for:** young people, fine diners, clubbers

### [Ginza/Hibiya](https://trulytokyo.com/ginza/)
Ginza and Hibiya are two districts a short walk south of the Tokyo Station Area. Hibiya is famous for its expansive park while Ginza is known as an upscale shopping and dining area. Both are great choices for those who want to avoid the really busy hubs like Shinjuku and Shibuya. And for easy access to transport, you’ll find it hard to beat these two attractive districts.
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** close to Tokyo Station, great shopping and dining
* **Negatives:** can feel more like a shopping district than a neighborhood
* **Best for:** older travelers, well-heeled travelers

### [Shimbashi, Shiodome and Hamamatsucho](https://trulytokyo.com/shimbashi-shiodome-hamamatsucho-and-shinagawa/)
A short distance south of Tokyo Station, these three adjoining districts are just far enough from Tokyo Station to mean cheaper hotel rates but close enough so that you can be there in a few minutes. And there are some good places to eat around here. I occasionally stay in either Hamamatsucho or Shimbashi because hotel rates are cheap here and it still feels very close to Tokyo Station (and Hamamatsucho is where you catch the monorail to Haneda).
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** convenient to Tokyo Station and Haneda Airport
* **Negatives:** relatively few shops and restaurants
* **Best for:** business travelers, solo travelers

### [Ebisu, Meguro and Daikanyama](https://trulytokyo.com/ebisu-daikanyama-and-meguro/)
Ebisu and Meguro are two small urban hubs on the southwest side of the Yamanote Line. Daikanyama is a small hub just west of Ebisu. These three areas are considered the most desirable places to live by Tokyo’s fashionable and wealthy elite. There are a few hotels in these areas that allow you to enjoy the things that locals love: great cafes, hip restaurants and bars, boutique shopping, and pleasant strolling on attractive streets.
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** fashionable, hip, low-key
* **Negatives:** expensive for shopping and dining
* **Best for:** solo travelers, couples, hipsters

### [Shinagawa](https://trulytokyo.com/shimbashi-shiodome-hamamatsucho-and-shinagawa/)
Shinagawa is a major transport hub at the southern end of the Yamanote Line. It’s not the most exciting district, but it’s right on the loop line and the Tokaido Shinkansen line, so it’s quite convenient to stay here (especially if you plan to hop on and off the shinkansen). There are several excellent hotels here that take advantage of the area’s excellent transport connections.
#### Key Points
* **Positives:** transport hub, direct train to Haneda Airport
* **Negatives:** not many things to see or do
* **Best for:** business travelers, frequent flyers

## Types Of Accommodation In Tokyo
Tokyo has an incredible range of places to stay. The city has some of the best hotels in the world (with prices to match) and yet there are tons of perfectly acceptable business hotels that cost far less than their equivalents in other developed countries. And, yes, there are ryokan (traditional Japanese inns) in Tokyo, but not that many of them (Kyoto is the place for ryokan).
### Hotels
Tokyo has a fantastic selection of hotels in all price ranges.
* The city’s luxury hotels are on par with the best hotels anywhere. For more information, check out the [Best Tokyo Luxury Hotels](https://trulytokyo.com/best-tokyo-luxury-hotels/) page.
* The best value can often be found in mid-range and so-called “business hotels,” which are small but efficient hotels that often offer great rates. For more information, check out the [Best Tokyo Mid-Range And Business Hotels](https://trulytokyo.com/best-tokyo-mid-range-and-business-hotels/) page.
* There is also a range of [boutique hotels in Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/best-tokyo-boutique-hotels/).
* Tokyo is a great city to visit with kids. But, where should you stay? See my [Best Tokyo Hotels For Families](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyos-best-hotels-for-families/) page.
* If you’re looking for a romantic hideaway in Tokyo, you’ll be spoiled for choice. See my list of [The Most Romantic Hotels In Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/the-most-romantic-hotels-in-tokyo/), all personally inspected.
* Finally, you might be surprised at just how cheap some of the city’s budget hotel rooms are. And, the quality is surprisingly high. For more information, check out the [Best Tokyo Budget Hotels](https://trulytokyo.com/best-tokyo-budget-hotels/) page.
Also see our overview on the [Tokyo Hotels](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyo-hotels/) page.
You can check current Tokyo hotel availability at [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/city/jp/tokyo.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?cid=1741664&pcs=1&city=5085&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
### Ryokan
Because Tokyo was bombed flat during WWII, there aren’t many traditional accommodations left in the city. However, you can still find some ryokan (traditional Japanese inns) in the city.
These are our picks for the best ryokan in Tokyo:
* **Tokyo Station Area:** [Hoshinoya Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/hoshinoya-tokyo/) - Hoshinoya Tokyo is the finest ryokan in Tokyo. Full stop. If you’re looking for Japanese-style luxury accommodation in a very convenient location, it’s a fantastic choice. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/hoshinoya-tokyo/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/hoshinoya-tokyo.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=1327906&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
* **Asakusa Budget:** [Ryokan Asakusa Mikawaya Honten](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-mikawaya/) - The Ryokan Mikawaya is a mid-range ryokan located a stone’s throw from Senso-ji Temple in Asakusa. It’s got spacious Japanese-style rooms which can easily accommodate families. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-mikawaya/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/ryokan-mikawaya.en-gb.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=181804&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
* **Asakusa Mid-Range:** [Ryokan Asakusa Shigetsu](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-asakusa-shigetsu/) - With a superb location just around the corner from Senso-ji Temple, the Asakusa Shigetsu is the best ryokan in Asakusa. It’s a great place to sample the ryokan experience in Tokyo. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-asakusa-shigetsu/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/ryokan-asakusa-shigetsu.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=292355&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
* **Asakusa Luxury:** [Ryokan Kamogawa Asakusa](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-kamogawa-asakusa/) - With a superb location in Asakusa, the moderately-priced Ryokan Kamogawa Asakusa is our favorite ryokan in Tokyo. The rooms are perfect for families who want to stay together. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-kamogawa-asakusa/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/ryokan-kamogawa-asakusa.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=248477&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
* **Ueno:** [Ryokan Katsutaro](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-katsutaro/) - A short walk from Ueno-koen Park, the Ryokan Katsutaro is a friendly little ryokan that offers traditional rooms at reasonable rates. Rooms here can accommodate up to four people, making it a good choice for families. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/ryokan-katsutaro/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/ryokan-katsutaro.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=570333&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
* **Yanaka:** [Annex Katsutaro Ryokan](https://trulytokyo.com/annex-katsutaro-ryokan/) - Located in Yanaka, one of Tokyo’s most traditional neighborhoods, this fine budget ryokan is the perfect place to sample the ryokan experience in Tokyo. It’s a modern building but the rooms are ryokan style. [Read our review](https://trulytokyo.com/annex-katsutaro-ryokan/) or view on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/annex-katsutaro-ryokan.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?CID=1741664&pcs=1&hid=295761&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
For more information, check out the [Tokyo Ryokan](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyo-ryokan/) page.
If you’re not going to visit Kyoto and you really want to try a night in a ryokan, then by all means, stay a night or two in a ryokan in Tokyo. But, if you are going to visit Kyoto, you might want to have your ryokan experience in Kyoto. For details, visit our [Kyoto Ryokan](https://www.insidekyoto.com/kyoto-ryokan) page.
### Guesthouses
In addition to hotels and ryokan, there are quite a few guesthouses in Tokyo that cater to budget travelers and backpackers. You’ll find most of them in the old parts of town like [Asakusa](https://trulytokyo.com/asakusa/). We recommend [K’s House Tokyo Oasis](https://trulytokyo.com/ks-house-tokyo-oasis-tokyo/).
### Capsule Hotels
You’ve probably heard of capsule hotels, those ultra-cheap and efficient accommodations designed for salarymen who’ve gotten drunk and missed the last train home. They usually consist of narrow coffin-like capsules and a common bathing area. Most are only for men, but there are a few that also accept women (and have women’s only floors). For our picks, check out the [Best Capsule Hotels In Tokyo](https://trulytokyo.com/best-capsule-hotels-in-tokyo/) page.
## Peak Season: March-April and November
**A FRIENDLY WARNING:** Tokyo accommodation gets booked out months in advance for busy seasons like the late March to mid-April [Tokyo cherry blossom season](https://trulytokyo.com/when-is-cherry-blossom-season-in-tokyo/) and the [November fall foliage season](https://trulytokyo.com/tokyos-best-fall-foliage-spots/). Book well in advance for these times.
You can check current Tokyo hotel availability during March, April and November on [Booking.com](https://www.booking.com/city/jp/tokyo.html?aid=965264;label=TT-where-to-stay) and [Agoda.com](https://www.agoda.com/partners/partnersearch.aspx?cid=1741664&pcs=1&city=5085&tag=TT-where-to-stay).
